questions.question: What is the target chest compression depth for an adult in ACLS?
options: A. At least 1 in (2.5 cm)
B. At least 2 in (5 cm), not more than 2.4 in (6 cm)
C. Exactly 3 in (7.5 cm)
D. As deep as possible with no upper limit
questions.rationale: Adult compression depth should be at least 2 in (5 cm) but not exceed 2.4 in (6 cm) to maximize blood flow while avoiding injury.

questions.question: What is chest compression fraction (CCF)?
options: A. The percentage of compressions delivered at correct depth
B. The proportion of total resuscitation time spent actually compressing the chest
C. The number of compressors used per code
D. The ratio of compressions to breaths
questions.rationale: CCF measures how much of the total resuscitation time compressions are actually occurring; a higher CCF (≥60%, ideally >80%) correlates with better outcomes.

questions.question: An advanced airway has just been successfully placed and confirmed during ongoing CPR. What ventilation strategy is now correct?
options: A. Continue 30:2 cycling
B. Continuous compressions with 1 breath every 6 seconds
C. Stop compressions to give 2 breaths every cycle
D. Ventilate as fast as possible
questions.rationale: Once an advanced airway is placed, compressions become continuous and a single breath is given every 6 seconds (~10/min), independent of the compression cycle.

questions.question: Why does over-ventilation harm a patient in cardiac arrest?
options: A. It has no physiologic effect
B. It raises intrathoracic pressure, reduces venous return, and lowers cardiac output
C. It improves oxygenation with no downside
D. It only matters after ROSC
questions.rationale: Excessive ventilation rate or volume increases intrathoracic pressure, impeding venous return to the heart and reducing the cardiac output generated by compressions.

questions.question: What is the recommended interval for switching chest compressors?
options: A. Every 30 seconds
B. About every 2 minutes, or sooner if fatigued
C. Only at the end of the code
D. Every 10 minutes
questions.rationale: Compressor fatigue degrades quality even before the rescuer feels tired, so switching roughly every 2 minutes (often paired with rhythm checks) preserves compression quality.

questions.question: A team leader says 'Someone give epinephrine.' What team dynamics principle is being violated?
options: A. Knowing your limitations
B. Closed-loop communication and clear, directed orders
C. Mutual respect
D. Reevaluation
questions.rationale: Orders should be directed to a specific named person and closed with a repeat-back and confirmation; vague, undirected orders risk being missed or duplicated.

questions.question: What is the correct sequence of closed-loop communication?
options: A. Order given to a named person → receiver repeats it back → receiver confirms completion
B. Order given generally to the room → no confirmation needed
C. Task performed first, then discussed
D. Only the leader speaks during the code
questions.rationale: Closed-loop communication requires a directed order, a verbal repeat-back, and confirmation once the task is complete.

questions.question: A junior team member notices the code leader about to order a second dose of a drug already given. What should they do?
options: A. Stay silent because the leader outranks them
B. Respectfully speak up immediately (constructive intervention)
C. Wait until after the code to mention it
D. Give the drug anyway without comment
questions.rationale: Constructive intervention means any team member should speak up promptly and respectfully when they notice a potential error, regardless of hierarchy.

questions.question: What is the primary role of a dedicated CPR coach during a resuscitation?
options: A. To administer all medications
B. To monitor and give real-time feedback on compression rate, depth, recoil, and pauses
C. To lead the code and make all treatment decisions
D. To document only
questions.rationale: A CPR coach focuses exclusively on compression quality metrics, allowing the team leader to focus on rhythm interpretation and overall management.

questions.question: What compression-to-ventilation ratio is used before an advanced airway is in place?
options: D. Continuous compressions with no breaths
questions.rationale: Without an advanced airway, adult CPR uses a 30:2 compression-to-breath ratio.

questions.question: During a code, compression pauses for rhythm checks are consistently running 15–20 seconds. What should the team do?
options: A. Accept it as normal
B. Identify the cause of the delay and shorten the pause to under 10 seconds
C. Stop checking rhythms altogether
D. Increase compression rate to compensate
questions.rationale: Pauses should be kept under 10 seconds; a team noticing longer pauses should identify and correct the cause (e.g., slow AED analysis, difficulty visualizing the rhythm) to protect CCF.

questions.question: Which best describes 'knowing your limitations' as a team dynamics element?
options: A. Refusing any task outside your comfort zone without explanation
B. Proactively communicating when a task is beyond your training so someone else can take over
C. Attempting every task regardless of skill level
D. Only the team leader needs to know their limitations
questions.rationale: Team members should openly communicate when a task exceeds their training or ability so responsibility can shift to someone more capable, preserving patient safety.

questions.question: What effect does chronic rescuer fatigue have on compression quality if compressors are not switched?
options: A. No measurable effect
B. Compression depth and rate quality decline, often before the rescuer subjectively feels tired
C. Only compression rate is affected, never depth
D. Fatigue improves consistency
questions.rationale: Studies show compression quality (especially depth) degrades with fatigue, often before the rescuer notices — hence the routine ~2-minute switch.

questions.question: Which statement about full chest recoil is correct?
options: A. Leaning on the chest between compressions improves blood flow
B. Full recoil between compressions allows the heart to refill and is required for effective CPR
C. Recoil is only relevant for pediatric patients
D. Recoil does not affect compression quality
questions.rationale: Full recoil between compressions permits venous return and cardiac filling; leaning on the chest reduces effective blood flow.

questions.question: A resuscitation team never verbally confirms medication doses given. What is the primary risk?
options: A. Improved efficiency
B. Missed doses or accidental duplicate dosing due to lost situational awareness
C. No risk if the leader remembers everything
D. Faster completion of the code
questions.rationale: Without closed-loop confirmation, the team can lose track of what has been given, risking missed or duplicated doses, especially in long or high-stress resuscitations.

questions.question: What best describes the relationship between CCF and outcomes?
options: A. CCF has no established relationship with survival
B. Higher CCF (≥60%, ideally >80%) is associated with better perfusion and outcomes
C. Lower CCF is preferred to allow more frequent pulse checks
D. CCF only matters after ROSC
questions.rationale: Chest compression fraction reflects how much of the resuscitation time compressions were actually delivered; higher CCF supports better perfusion and is linked with improved outcomes.

questions.question: Why should ventilation rate be independent of the compression cycle once an advanced airway is placed?
options: A. Because compressions must pause for each breath
B. Because compressions are continuous and breaths (1 every 6 seconds) are delivered asynchronously without pausing compressions
C. Because ventilation is no longer needed
D. Because breaths should match the compression rate exactly
questions.rationale: With an advanced airway, ventilations (1 every 6 seconds) are delivered independently while compressions continue uninterrupted.

questions.question: A new nurse is unsure how to titrate a vasopressor infusion ordered mid-code. What is the correct action per team dynamics principles?
options: A. Guess and proceed without saying anything
B. Clearly state the limitation to the team leader so someone qualified can take over or assist
C. Refuse to participate in the code at all
D. Delay the whole code until they figure it out alone
questions.rationale: Knowing your limitations means openly communicating a skills gap so the task can be reassigned or supported, keeping the resuscitation safe and efficient.
